title: My backtests keep lying to me and I've run out of ways to catch it myself
author: xXDADDYTHRASHERXx
selftext:
Been building a forex bot for about a year, a lot of it with AI doing the actual coding while I drive strategy and validation. Small live account, started at $330, now \~$246. The wall I keep hitting: backtest says one thing, live does the opposite. Not "a little worse." Opposite sign. The thing that backtests at profit factor 1.6 does 0.5 with real money.



I assumed overfitting and threw validation at it. Helped a bit. The real culprits were dumber:



\- Sim ≠ live. My backtester and live bot weren't the same code, and they disagreed in tiny ways. A size cap that silently halved my real risk. Risk multipliers compounding live but flat in sim. A config merge quietly re-enabling something I'd "deleted." Found fifteen of these. Together they turned a +11%/month backtest into a real loss.



\- A look-ahead bug that looked like genius. An EMA cross beat a random-entry baseline \~100% of runs. Turned out it was deciding on a bar's close and filling at that same bar's open, i.e. trading on info it wouldn't have had yet. Fixed the fill, edge gone (PF 1.2 → 0.6).



\- Costs eat the simple stuff. Buy-the-dip, sell-a-pop, basic pullbacks on the majors all needed an \~80% win rate just to clear the spread. None got close. Confirmed three different ways.



Before anything touches money now: it has to beat a random-entry AND a dumb EMA-cross baseline on the same exits, survive walk-forward across windows, and I track time-underwater instead of just max drawdown. Everything shadow-trades first. Currently reading up on the Deflated Sharpe Ratio for the "tried 50 things, kept the best" inflation.



Two real questions:



\- For anyone who actually crossed the backtest-to-live gap on a small account: what specifically mattered? Not "more data."



\- Is "simple price edges don't clear costs on the majors anymore" just the answer in 2026, or am I testing the wrong instruments? Where does retail edge actually live now, AI/ML or otherwise?



Not after a magic indicator. More interested in how experienced people think about this. Roast it if it deserves it.

comments.author: ATKInvestments
comments.body: Im doing a bunch of backtesting currently and for the last 6 months. The ai you use and service level matters alot. I currently use codex on pro plan and the Ai is much smarter. But the output isn't usable on its own. I have to manually change it. But the strategy and structure that the Ai produces is something I wouldn't have stumbled on my own. If somebody knows something better? Im listening.

replies.author: xXDADDYTHRASHERXx
replies.body:
This resonates. Same here  the AI (Claude in my case) gives me structure and combos I wouldn't have reached on my own, but I can't ship its output as-is either. It'll hand me a gorgeous backtest with look-ahead bias baked right in and look proud of it.



What actually moved the needle for me wasn't a smarter model, it was building a dumb validator that doesn't care how clever the code looks. Every strategy it writes has to beat a random entry baseline and a basic EMA cross baseline on the same exits, or it's out. The model that writes the strategy is the same one that'll fool you, so the guardrail has to sit outside it.



What does your validation look like  do you let the model grade its own backtest, or is something independent checking it? That's where most of my hidden losses were living.

replies.author: ATKInvestments
replies.body: I use a indicator i created with Ai in a separate session as baseline and then have ai do backtesting to beat baseline. I then add the element ai beat baseline with to the indicator creating a new variation and start all over again. Slow But powerful.

replies.author: xXDADDYTHRASHERXx
replies.body:
That's a cool loop and I respect that it's slow, most people won't sit with something that long. One thing I'd watch though. If every round you're testing the new variation against the same baseline on the same history, you can curve fit to that data without ever feeling it. Each element you keep because it "beat the baseline" might just be fitting noise that happened to live in that particular price history.

That's almost exactly the hole I fell into. I knew I was overfitting somewhere but couldn't prove where. What finally caught it was forcing each candidate onto a chunk of data it had never seen, plus making it beat dumb baselines on the same exits, not just the same indicator. If the new element still wins on fresh history it never trained on, that's when I'd trust it.

Are you holding out any out-of-sample data between rounds, or is the baseline the only thing that stays fixed?

replies.author: ATKInvestments
replies.body:
I try everything. 

But I take a visual look on a chart software before moving on to backtesting something new. That way I can verify the backtesting outcome and make any adjustments I need before making the new variant baseline. And starting over again. I have a goal and I'm  getting to a point were the indicator is as good as it possibly can get. You hit a point were you hit limits on whatever you're working on. Have you ran your indicator/strategy through a Monte Carlo test?

replies.author: xXDADDYTHRASHERXx
replies.body:
Not yet, and funny enough it's the literal next thing on my list. The plan is to resample my trade history a few thousand times to get a range on the profit factor instead of a single number, because on a small sample that single number lies to you with a straight face.

On the visual chart check before backtesting, I do something similar but I stay paranoid about it. Eyeballing a chart I tend to see what I want to see, so I try to use it only to sanity check the numbers, not to talk myself into a setup they don't support. And "getting the indicator as good as it can possibly get" is the one part I'd gently poke at, since as good as it can get on the data you have can also just mean maximally fit to that exact history. Monte Carlo plus a chunk of data it's never seen is how I try to tell the difference. How are you deciding you've hit the real limit versus just the limit of that dataset?

replies.author: ATKInvestments
replies.body: The settings on my indicator starts making the indicator signal late. So the 2 options are: signal early with more false signals overall or signal later with less false signals. That's why I, indicator stack in the first place. The monte carlo test shows me the worst possible outcome with a strategy. And then I can decide if I can live with that or not.

replies.author: xXDADDYTHRASHERXx
replies.body:
yeah that early/late tradeoff is exactly what bit me. i tried to make one of my setups fire earlier by entering on a limit instead of waiting for the candle to close, figured i'd catch a better price. stop-outs jumped from like 38% to 44% and the whole thing went from down 587 pips to down 1223. waiting later = fewer bad entries, just like you said. that extra confirmation candle was paying for itself and i didn't realize it til i broke it.

the monte carlo thing is the gap i'm trying to close right now. i've been doing walk-forward and baseline comparisons but i've never actually resampled to see the worst case. when you run it, are you shuffling the trade order or resampling the returns? and do you key off worst drawdown or more like % of runs that stay profitable?

replies.author: ATKInvestments
replies.body: After you run it. AI should tell you those answers. As that's the reason for the test, is to know worst case scenarios. It helps with confidence in you're trading because then you know absolute worst case scenario and it doesn't tear you don't emotional if it happens in live trading. My system is 67%accurate with 3 to 4 losses before win possible. But worst case is 6 to 7 stopped out losses in a row.

comments.author: d_e_g_m
comments.body: You went from backtest to live without forward test period?

replies.author: xXDADDYTHRASHERXx
replies.body: Yeah  walk-forward. I split the history into rolling windows and the strategy has to hold up across most of them, not just look good in aggregate or lean on one lucky stretch. That's the first filter. Then it paper trades on live data in real time oanda demo account before the live account risks a cent. Walk forward kills the curve fits; the live paper stage catches the sim vs live execution stuff no historical test can see.

replies.author: xXDADDYTHRASHERXx
replies.body: What's the pitch for powakadata  what does it get you over your broker's own feed? I'm wary of testing on price data that isn't from the venue I actually execute on, since the fills never end up matching reality. If it solves something past just "more data," I'm listening.

replies.author: FantasticShine4012
replies.body:
not a replacement for your broker's feed for   
live execution — you're right that fills will   
never match. but for strategy research across   
multiple instruments the broker feed has gaps   
and DST issues that skew results. powakadata   
is cleaned, verified, consistent across sources.   
useful for the research phase, not the execution   
phase. powakadata.com if you want to check the   
sample data

comments.author: Thwerty
comments.body: You have found out the fundamental problem with back testing. What is your strategy

replies.author: xXDADDYTHRASHERXx
replies.body:
Honestly it's not one strategy, it's a few small ones that each only get to trade in the condition they actually tested well in. Mean reversion scalps when a pair is ranging, breakout and trend stuff when it's genuinely trending, S/R fades at levels. A regime filter decides which one, if any, is allowed to fire on a given pair right now. Most of the time the answer is none of them, which is kind of the whole point.

They're all running in shadow mode at the moment, logging what they would've done without risking the live account, while I build up enough real samples to actually trust them.

You said it like you've already made peace with the backtesting problem. What's your read on it, beatable or just something you learn to manage?

replies.author: Thwerty
replies.body:
Well, I don't look at chart patterns as a deciding factor to take a trade, though certainly check them for general idea of the stock. 

 I wouldn't recommend trying to make it work for scalping.  I find it works much better for swing trade, entry exit is not critical that way.

Backtesting is important yes but as a general feasibility on a strategy. training an algo based on events that already happened doesn't prepare it for the future. If it did, everyone would be winning which is not possible, someone has to lose, it's proof by logic. Market doesn't work that way. And any edge you find will be short lived due to this nature, it's going to need constant tweaking. It's better to have a generalist approach, taking multiple signals and conditions, then slowly tighten your conditions, tweak only one thing at a time always. And never blindly trust the code AI gives you (software engineer here)

replies.author: xXDADDYTHRASHERXx
replies.body:
This is a good push and I think you're right about the scalping. The tighter the trade, the more the spread and fees dominate, and I've got the numbers to back you up. The simple short-horizon stuff I tested needed something like an 80% win rate just to clear costs, which is absurd. Swing gives the move room to pay for the friction. Fair hit.

Where I'd push back a little is on "constant tweaking." That's the exact trap I fell into. I tweaked and tweaked and a lot of what I was chasing turned out to be noise, not a decaying edge. So now I'm stuck on the harder question, how do you tell an edge that genuinely decayed and needs a tune from a thing that was never real and should just be killed? Tweaking one variable at a time, yes, fully agree, that part I already do.

And "never blindly trust the AI code" is basically the whole reason I posted. As an engineer, how do you draw the line on where the AI helps versus where you take the wheel?

replies.author: Thwerty
replies.body: .. Is that claude.

replies.author: xXDADDYTHRASHERXx
replies.body: I use Claude and codex mostly Claude and then codex to look for things Claude may have missed

replies.author: Thwerty
replies.body: I meant your reply is claude lol

replies.author: xXDADDYTHRASHERXx
replies.body: Ah yes lol.  I am horrible at  spelling and grammar so I have Claude clean it up for me ha ha

replies.author: Thwerty
replies.body:
Idk if it's good or bad I can tell what models wrote any text lol.

To answer your question, There is no line drawing. you have to validate the logic by reading the code and writing proper tests. Without having more information I can't tell you if and where you may have logical errors or fundamental errors. 

I don't use algo to trade, I use it for signals and alerts that match my conditions, on my self curated watchlist, then I take the wheel and make the decision. Don't try to cast a big net.

replies.author: Thwerty
replies.body: And as @atkinvestments said, make sure you are using a clean market data and reputable services. Not tradingview for example.

replies.author: xXDADDYTHRASHERXx
replies.body:
ha fair, yeah i run my replies through claude to fix my spelling, my grammar's rough. the thinking's mine though, for better or worse.



the read-the-code-and-test part i learned the hard way. had a backtest that looked great and it turned out it was peeking at the close of a candle to decide the entry, then filling at the open of that same candle. free money that doesn't exist live. only caught it by hand-checking one trade start to finish. now i hand-verify a trade on every new test before i believe any of it.



and the "don't cast a big net" thing hit home. i started across 9 pairs with every strategy on and it was just mush. been cutting it down to only trade certain setups in certain market conditions instead. the signals-and-alerts-then-you-take-the-wheel approach is interesting to me, how'd you land on that over letting it execute? trust thing, or did you just find you read certain situations better than the rules did?

on the data, i'm pulling straight from my broker's price feed (oanda) so the prices i test on are the same source i'd actually fill on. figured that beats a third party that's been cleaned or interpolated. what are you using? you said reputable but didn't say who, curious what you trust for the clean stuff.

replies.author: xXDADDYTHRASHERXx
replies.body:
ha fair, the read the code and test part i learned the hard way. had a backtest that looked great and it turned out it was peeking at the close of a candle to decide the entry, then filling at the open of that same candle. free money that doesn't exist live. only caught it by hand checking one trade start to finish. now i hand verify a trade on every new test before i believe any of it.

and the "don't cast a big net" thing hit home. i started across 9 pairs with every strategy on and it was just mush. been cutting it down to only trade certain setups in certain market conditions instead. the signals and alerts then you take the wheel approach is interesting to me, how'd you land on that over letting it execute? trust thing, or did you just find you read certain situations better than the rules did?

comments.author: Minimum_Raccoon_1501
comments.body: You can always split your backrest data into training data and test data.  Several ways to do that. Only train on the everything but last two years.  Then run the simulation on the last two years to see how well it runs on untrained data instead of waiting to the performance live.   Also,  maybe even pull out specific years like a bull market, bear market, sideways year.  Then train the model on everything but those years.   If you model does bad in bear years and the live data is during a bad year then the model is performing correctly.

replies.author: xXDADDYTHRASHERXx
replies.body:
That's pretty much what walk forward does for me, rolling train/test splits across the history so it has to hold up on data it wasn't fit on, not just in aggregate. The piece I like in your version is pulling out a whole regime on purpose, hold back the trending stretch, train on the rest, and check that it fails where it should. Expecting it to do badly in the regime it never saw, and being happy when it does, is a nice flip on the usual instinct.

Forex doesn't hand me clean multi-year bull and bear blocks like stocks do, but it does give me ranging versus trending regimes, and my live bot actually gates each strategy to the regime it tested well in. So holding out by regime in testing maps almost one to one onto how it runs live. Appreciate that, might tighten how I slice the windows.

replies.author: Minimum_Raccoon_1501
replies.body: You should look on kaggle. You can probably down load the whole entire nasdaq history in one file.

replies.author: xXDADDYTHRASHERXx
replies.body: appreciate it. i'm on forex not stocks so the nasdaq set won't slot right in, but i didn't know kaggle had whole histories in one file like that, handy to know. have you actually pulled data off there? heard the quality's hit or miss depending who uploaded it.

replies.author: Minimum_Raccoon_1501
replies.body: The data on Kaggle is solid.  It’s for contests in the data analytics community.  I yanked the whole Bitcoin minute candles from there recently

comments.author: French_Brief8289
comments.body:
Hey  
I had the same problem until I came across one hidden problem : slippage & spread.   
I changed my broker for a more consistent broker with a 100ms execution time max and no spread (ECN broker) and it works well. 

Which broker do you use?

replies.author: xXDADDYTHRASHERXx
replies.body:
OANDA, on a small live account. And you're pointing right at my sore spot, spread and slippage are exactly what's been eating the simple strategies I test. OANDA is a spread markup model rather than raw spread plus commission, so on tight trades the cost really bites.

The ECN angle is a lever I honestly haven't pulled yet, and on a small account the commission versus spread math matters a lot, so it's worth me modeling properly before I switch anything. Which broker did you move to, and roughly what are you paying in commission per round turn? Trying to work out if the switch pencils out at my size.

replies.author: French_Brief8289
replies.body:
It depends on the symbol. X100 leverage:  
But usually,   
For 1 EURJPY, 3$.   
For 1 XAUUSD, 3$ too

comments.author: Ready_Log4016
comments.body: That's a progress. Most people never catch why their backtesting is lying. A backtest only matters if it survives cost, slippage, walk forward, and live execution. I'd stop optimizing random strats and test one idea properly. I use backtesting on blueblocx and i find it more reliable.

replies.author: xXDADDYTHRASHERXx
replies.body:
Yeah, that's exactly where I landed. Those four are the gauntlet, and live execution is the one that wrecked me hardest. My backtester and my live bot weren't literally the same code, so they drifted in small ways that added up to flipping the sign on my returns.

I keep my research data and my execution path totally separate now and treat any strategy as lying until it clears all four. The "test one idea properly instead of optimizing ten random ones" part is the discipline I had to learn the hard way. What does testing one idea properly look like for you, how long do you sit with it before you call it?

comments.author: Think_Soft2282
comments.body: the sim/live code split is the actual killer and nobody talks about it enough. i caught something similar when i was running parallel tests on Trade Quo (cfd broker, has mt4/mt5) and my local backtest, the fill logic alone accounted for like 40% of the gap before i even looked at the strategy. profit factor means nothing if the two environments aren't running identical execution assumptions

comments.author: nashwan888
comments.body: With such a small account you are probably losing a lot on noise. You need money to make money! Try starting on 5k instead.

replies.author: xXDADDYTHRASHERXx
replies.body:
i hear you on the variance, $250 is way too small to sit through a losing streak without the drawdown looking ugly in percent terms. that part's real.

where i'm not sure though, on forex the costs scale with position size, so the spread eats the same fraction of my edge whether i'm trading $250 or $5k. so if the edge is actually negative after costs, a bigger account just loses it faster in dollars. my problem looks more like the edge than the account size right now. when you went up to 5k did it actually change your results, or was it more that you could size properly and ride out the swings?

replies.author: nashwan888
replies.body: My bot closes a lot of trades on noise when my position was too small. It's still in development and not profitable. I have noticed that it won't close on noise on bigger position but that can also lead to bigger losses!

comments.author: Bunkport
comments.body: Have your bot look for coe inconsistency and the run montecarlo simulation say 10k times

replies.author: xXDADDYTHRASHERXx
replies.body: yeah the code consistency part is no joke, worst bug i had was the backtest just disagreeing with what the live bot would actually do, took me forever to find. montecarlo 10k runs is literally the next thing on my list. when you run yours what do you actually pull out of it, worst drawdown, the spread of profit factors, % of runs that end green? trying to figure out what number to actually trust before i build it.
